items.question: How are ground and/or shield planes treated when evaluating adjacent pattern spacing for a contained imperfection in flex circuitry?
items.sft_question: How are ground and/or shield planes treated when evaluating adjacent pattern spacing for a contained imperfection in flex circuitry?
items.answer: Ground and/or shield planes are treated as one conductive pattern and do not apply to adjacent pattern spacing when the imperfection is contained completely within common conductors.

evidence.quote: Note: Ground and/or shield planes are treated as one conductive pattern and do not apply to adjacent pattern spacing when the imperfection is contained completely within common conductors.

items.question: What is the requirement when cuts, nicks, gouges, tears, or other physical damage affect the thickness of flexible material?
items.sft_question: What is the requirement when cuts, nicks, gouges, tears, or other physical damage affect the thickness of flexible material?
items.answer: They shall not result in exposed circuitry.

evidence.quote: Cuts, nicks, gouges, tears or other physical damage affecting the flex material thickness shall not result in exposed circuitry.

items.question: What edge damage is acceptable for Class 1 versus Class 2 and 3 flexible printed circuitry on this page?
items.sft_question: What edge damage is acceptable for Class 1 versus Class 2 and 3 flexible printed circuitry on this page?
items.answer: For Class 1, nicks or damage along edges and cutouts are acceptable if the penetration does not exceed 50% of the distance from the edge to the nearest conductor or 2.5 mm [0.1 in], whichever is less. For Class 2 and 3, no nicks, tears, or imperfections along the flexible edges are acceptable.

evidence.quote: Acceptable - Class 1 - Nicks or damage along the edges of the flexible printed circuitry and cutouts, providing the penetration does not exceed 50% of the distance from the edge to the nearest conductor or 2.5 mm [0.1 in], whichever is less. Acceptable - Class 2,3 - No nicks, tears or imperfections along the flexible edges of the flexible printed circuitry.

items.question: For Class 2, what is the maximum acceptable extent of solder wicking or plating migration under the coverlayer?

evidence.quote: Acceptable - Class 2 - Solder wicking/plating migration does not extend under coverlayer more than 0.5 mm [0.02 in].

items.question: What condition is a defect for all Classes 1, 2, and 3 in flexible and rigid-flex printed circuitry solder wicking?

items.answer: It is a defect if solder wicking or plating migration extends into the area required to be flexible, or if the resulting spacing violates minimum electrical clearance.

evidence.quote: Defect - Class 1,2,3 - Solder wicking or plating migration extends into the area required to be flexible. - Spacing as a result of solder wicking or plating migration violates minimum electrical clearance.

items.question: What types of flexible circuitry attachment are the criteria on this page currently applicable to?
items.sft_question: What types of flexible circuitry attachment are the criteria on this page currently applicable to?
items.answer: They are applicable to the solder attachment of flex on PCB (FOB). The text also says the section may later be expanded to include flex on flex (FOF) and anisotropically conductive flex (ACF) connections.

evidence.quote: 10.4.4 Flexible and Rigid-Flex Printed Circuitry - Attachment These criteria are applicable to the solder attachment of flex on PCB (FOB). When sufficient data has been collected this will be expanded to include flex on flex (FOF) and connection using anisotropically conductive flex (ACF).

items.question: What is the maximum allowed side overhang of a flex termination for Class 1 versus Class 2 and 3?
items.sft_question: What is the maximum allowed side overhang of a flex termination for Class 1 versus Class 2 and 3?
items.answer: For Class 1, side overhang is acceptable when it is equal to or less than 50% of the flex termination width. For Class 2 and 3, it is acceptable when it is equal to or less than 25% of the flex termination width.

evidence.quote: Acceptable - Class 1 - Side overhang of flex termination is equal to or less than 50% of flex termination width. Acceptable - Class 2,3 - Side overhang of flex termination is equal to or less than 25% of flex termination width.

items.question: According to IPC-A-610G section 10.5, what must happen to assembly markings such as part numbers and serial numbers after testing, cleaning, and other processes?
items.sft_question: According to IPC-A-610G section 10.5, what must happen to assembly markings such as part numbers and serial numbers after testing, cleaning, and other processes?
items.answer: Assembly markings such as part numbers and serial numbers shall remain legible after all tests, cleaning, and other processes to which the item is subjected.

evidence.quote: Assembly marking (part numbers, serial numbers) shall remain legible (capable of being read and understood as defined by the requirements of this Standard) after all tests, cleaning and other processes to which the item is subjected.

items.question: What types of assembly surfaces are included in the cleanliness acceptability requirements in this section?

items.answer: The cleanliness requirements include assemblies with components that have electrical interfacing surfaces, such as connector mating surfaces and compliant pins.

items.question: What test methods are recommended for establishing a facility standard for tolerable contaminant levels?
items.sft_question: What test methods are recommended for establishing a facility standard for tolerable contaminant levels?
items.answer: Recommended methods include testing with ionic extract devices based on J-STD-001, insulation resistance tests under environmental conditions, and other electrical parameter tests described in IPC-TM-650.

evidence.quote: Testing with ionic extract devices based on J-STD-001, insulation resistance tests under environmental conditions and other electrical parameter tests as described in IPC-TM-650 are recommended for setting a facility standard.

items.question: What is the function of the solder mask during solder assembly operation?

items.question: Where should conformal coating thickness be measured according to IPC-A-610G page 406?
items.sft_question: Where should conformal coating thickness be measured according to IPC-A-610G page 406?
items.answer: It should be measured on a flat, unencumbered, cured surface of the printed circuit assembly or on a coupon that has been processed with the assembly.

evidence.quote: The thickness is to be measured on a flat, unencumbered, cured surface of the printed circuit assembly or a coupon that has been processed with the assembly.

items.question: What is the acceptability criterion for conformal coating thickness for Class 1, 2, and 3 assemblies?
items.sft_question: What is the acceptability criterion for conformal coating thickness for Class 1, 2, and 3 assemblies?
items.answer: For Classes 1, 2, and 3, the coating is acceptable if it meets the thickness requirements of Table 10-1; it is a defect if it does not meet those requirements.

evidence.quote: Acceptable - Class 1,2,3 - Coating meets the thickness requirements of Table 10-1. Defect - Class 1,2,3 - Coating does not meet thickness requirements of Table 10-1.

items.question: What methods may be used to examine conformal coating coverage on an assembly?

items.answer: The assembly may be examined with the unaided eye. If the material contains a fluorescent pigment, blacklight may be used to verify coverage, and white light may be used as an aid for examining coverage.

items.question: What general visual characteristics should conformal coatings have on electronic assemblies?
items.sft_question: What general visual characteristics should conformal coatings have on electronic assemblies?
items.answer: Conformal coatings should be transparent, uniform in color and consistency, and should uniformly cover the board and components.

evidence.quote:
10.8.1 Conformal Coating - General
Conformal coatings should be transparent, uniform in color and consistency and uniformly cover the board and components.

items.question: What is not acceptable when trying to correct a defective solderless wrap connection?
items.sft_question: What is not acceptable when trying to correct a defective solderless wrap connection?
items.answer: It is not acceptable to correct a defective connection by reapplying the wrapping tool or by applying other tools.

evidence.quote: It is not acceptable to attempt to correct a defective connection by reapplying the wrapping tool or by applying other tools.

items.question: How should a defective solderless wrap connection be handled instead of repairing it by soldering?
items.sft_question: How should a defective solderless wrap connection be handled instead of repairing it by soldering?
items.answer: It should not be repaired by soldering. The defective connection is unwrapped using a special tool, then a new wire is wrapped to the terminal. A new wire must be used for each wrap or rewrap, though the terminal may be rewrapped many times.

evidence.quote: The reliability and maintainability advantages of the solderless wrap connection method are such that no repair of a defective wrap by soldering is to be made. Defective connections are unwrapped using a special tool (not stripped off the terminal) and then a new wire wrapped to the terminal. New wire shall be used for each wrap/rewrap, but the terminal may be rewrapped many times.

items.question: What is the acceptance criterion for wire slack in solderless wrap wiring?

items.answer: Wiring needs to have sufficient slack so that it will not pull around corners of the other terminal posts or bridge and load other wires.

evidence.quote: Wiring needs to have sufficient slack so that it will not pull around corners of the other terminal posts or bridge and load other wires.

evidence.quote: Acceptable - Class 1,2,3

items.question: For solderless wrap wire dress, what routing condition makes the wire dress acceptable for Class 1, 2, and 3?
items.sft_question: For solderless wrap wire dress, what routing condition makes the wire dress acceptable for Class 1, 2, and 3?
items.answer: The wire dress is acceptable when it is oriented so that axial force on the wire will not tend to unwrap the connection or relieve the wire bite on the terminal post corners; this is satisfied when the wire is routed so as to cross the 45° line as shown.

evidence.quote: Acceptable - Class 1,2,3 - The dress of wire is oriented so that force exerted axially on the wire will not tend to unwrap the connection, or to relieve the bite of wire on the corners of the terminal post. This requirement is satisfied when the wire is routed so as to cross the 45° line as shown.

items.question: What is the defect condition for solderless wrap wire dress for Class 1, 2, and 3?
items.sft_question: What is the defect condition for solderless wrap wire dress for Class 1, 2, and 3?
items.answer: It is a defect when axially exerted external forces on the wrap will cause the wrap to unwind or loosen the wire bite at the post corners.

evidence.quote: Defect - Class 1,2,3 - Axially exerted external forces on the wrap will cause the wrap to unwind or loosen the wire bite at the post corners.

items.question: What does IPC-2221 category A6 apply to?
items.sft_question: What does IPC-2221 category A6 apply to?
items.answer: Category A6 applies to external component leads and terminations that are not conformal coated, and they require the electrical clearances stated in that category.

evidence.quote: IPC-2221 - 6.3.6 A6-External Component Lead/Termination, Uncoated, Sea Level to 3050 m [10,007 feet] External component leads and terminations, that are not conformal coated, require electrical clearances stated in this category.

items.question: According to the note on this page, what must be completely coated to ensure the electrical clearance requirements for coated conductors in this category?
items.sft_question: According to the note on this page, what must be completely coated to ensure the electrical clearance requirements for coated conductors in this category?
items.answer: All conductors except for soldering lands must be completely coated.

evidence.quote: Note: All conductors, except for soldering lands, shall be completely coated in order to ensure the electrical clearance requirements in this category for coated conductors.

items.question: In Image 0, according to Table 6-1, what is the minimum spacing for category A6 when the voltage between conductors is 51-100 V?
items.sft_question: In the provided image, according to Table 6-1, what is the minimum spacing for category A6 when the voltage between conductors is 51-100 V?

evidence.visual_evidence: In the table, locate the row labeled 51-100 and the A6 column under Assembly; the cell shows 0.5 mm [0.020 in].

caption: Table 6-1 Electrical Conductor Spacing
footnote: Note 1. These values presume woven fiberglass coated with epoxy-based resin systems; other sytems may have different values.

items.question: Under section 6.5.1 Stress Relief - Bundle, which figure is identified as a defect for all classes because it does not meet bend radius requirements?
items.sft_question: Under section 6.5.1 Stress Relief - Bundle, which figure is identified as a defect for all classes because it does not meet bend radius requirements?
items.answer: Figure 6-59 is identified as a defect for Class 1, 2, and 3 because it does not meet bend radius requirements.

evidence.quote: Defect - Class 1,2,3 - Does not meet bend radius requirements. See Table 4-1, see Figure 6-59.

items.question: What is the acceptability classification shown for Figure 6-58?
items.sft_question: What is the acceptability classification shown for Figure 6-58?
items.answer: Figure 6-58 is Acceptable for Class 1, a Process Indicator for Class 2, and a Defect for Class 3.

evidence.quote: Acceptable - Class 1 Process Indicator - Class 2 Defect - Class 3

items.question: In Image 0, what feature is highlighted as noncompliant, leading to a defect classification for all classes?
items.sft_question: In the provided image, what feature is highlighted as noncompliant, leading to a defect classification for all classes?
items.answer: The wire bend radius is too tight and does not meet the required bend radius, so it is a defect for Class 1, 2, and 3.

text_evidence.quote: Defect - Class 1,2,3 - Does not meet bend radius requirements. See Table 4-1, see Figure 6-59.

evidence.visual_evidence: Image 0 shows a sharply bent red wire with the bend radius marked as 'r' and arrows emphasizing the bend area.

caption: Figure 6-59

items.sft_question: In the provided image, how is the wire bundle stress relief arrangement classified?
items.answer: It is acceptable for Class 1, 2, and 3.

evidence.visual_evidence: Image 1 shows a bundled cable with multiple looped stress-relief bends secured by ties or clamps.

caption: Figure 6-57

items.question: In Image 2, what is the class status of the illustrated stress relief configuration?
items.sft_question: In the provided image, what is the class status of the illustrated stress relief configuration?
items.answer: It is acceptable for Class 1, a process indicator for Class 2, and a defect for Class 3.

text_evidence.quote: Acceptable - Class 1 Process Indicator - Class 2 Defect - Class 3

items.question: For jumper wires attached to components other than axial leaded, how should the wire be attached?
items.sft_question: For jumper wires attached to components other than axial leaded, how should the wire be attached?
items.answer: The wire should be lap soldered to the component lead.

evidence.quote: For jumper wires attached to components other than axial leaded, lap solder the wire to the component lead.

items.question: For hook solder connections with leads wrapped less than 180°, what wetting requirement is acceptable for Class 1 and 2?
items.sft_question: For hook solder connections with leads wrapped less than 180°, what wetting requirement is acceptable for Class 1 and 2?
items.answer: Solder must be wetted to 100% of the contact area between the wire/lead and terminal interface.

evidence.quote: Acceptable - Class 1,2 - Solder is wetted to 100% of the contact area between the wire/lead and terminal interface for leads wrapped less than 180°.

items.question: When a hook connection wrap is equal to or more than 180°, what minimum fillet coverage becomes a defect for all classes?
items.sft_question: When a hook connection wrap is equal to or more than 180°, what minimum fillet coverage becomes a defect for all classes?
items.answer: Less than 75% fillet of the lead to terminal contact is a defect for Class 1, 2, and 3.

evidence.quote: Defect - Class 1,2,3 - Less than 75% fillet of the lead to terminal contact when the wrap is equal to or more than 180°.

items.sft_question: In the provided image, what acceptability category does this hook-solder example represent?

items.question: In Image 1, is the hook-solder example shown as a target or an acceptable condition?

text_evidence.quote: Acceptable - Class 1,2 - Solder is wetted to 100% of the contact area between the wire/lead and terminal interface for leads wrapped less than 180°.

evidence.visual_evidence: The joint appears soldered but not as fully idealized as the target example, and this figure is placed with the acceptable criteria.
